segments.s572-g9: بدیدند is a third-person plural past verb 'they saw' (simple past). هرگونه here functions adverbially 'in every way / by every route'. بازآمدند is a third-person plural past 'they returned / came back' — the three words form a short clause 'they saw and returned in every way'.
segments.s572-g10: بر + شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to / before the king' (بر used as 'toward/to'). گردن‌فراز is a descriptive epithet 'proud' placed after the noun. آمدند is third-person plural past 'they came' — together 'they came before the proud king'.
segments.s572-g11: که introduces a subordinate clause here ('for / that / because'): قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar'. ز in this classical/poetic use means 'from' or 'because of' and governs the following gerund-like phrase می‌خوردن ('drinking/eating' used as an activity). و connects to از شکار 'and from the hunt' — listing Caesar's occupations.
segments.s572-g12: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here ('indeed / at all / continuously') placed before the verb; هیچ is negative 'nothing / at all'. نندیشد is third-person singular past negative of اندیشیدن ('he did not think'); in epic Persian the negative prefix ن- appears before the present/past stem. از کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'about / of the battle'.
segments.s572-g13: سپاهش is the noun سپاه 'army' with the enclitic possessive ش 'his/its' (written separately in the annotation). پراگنده is a predicate adjective 'scattered'. از هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'from every direction/side' describing how the army was dispersed.
segments.s572-g14: به تاراج کردن is a purposive/verbal-noun phrase: به + noun تاراج ('pillage') plus کردن (light verb) yields 'to pillage' or 'for plundering'. The following به هر پهلوی is a directional phrase 'into/at every flank' (پهلوی = flank/side). The repeated به marks direction/purpose and links the plundering action to the various flanks.
segments.s572-g15: نه ... نه sets up a correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. روزش طلایه: روزش ('his day') + nominal predicate طلایه 'vanguard' — 'no vanguard in his day'. شب پاسبان parallels it 'nor night a watch/guard' (پاسبان as predicate). The possessive ش is attached to روز to indicate 'his day'.
segments.s572-g16: سپاهش همه = 'his whole army' (سپاه + enclitic ش, then همه 'all'). چون introduces a simile 'like', followed by رمه 'a flock'. بی‌شبان is a compound adjective 'without a shepherd / shepherdless' used predicatively: 'like a shepherdless flock'.
segments.s572-g1: بسی (an adverb) modifies the verb phrase and means 'much/often' here; برنیامد is a negative past verb meaning 'did not occur/appear' (the action did not happen); برین روزگار is a locative/temporal phrase where بر + این (برین) = 'in these' and روزگار = 'times' or 'days', so the whole chunk means 'it did not often happen in these days'. The order is poetic: adverb + negative verb + prepositional/time phrase.
segments.s572-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); شد is the past of شدن meaning 'became' or 'came to be'; مردم is the noun 'people/men' and لشکری is an adjective/nominal form meaning 'military' or 'an armed force' used here as the predicate (i.e. 'the people became an army'); the numeral phrase شش هزار (six thousand) attaches to لشکری to specify its size.
segments.s572-g3: فرستاد is a past tense verb 'sent' (verb first for poetic effect) and شاپور is the agent/name 'Shapur' (here following the verb); کارآگهان is a plural noun meaning 'the informed/knowledgeable ones' and functions as the direct object of فرستاد.
segments.s572-g4: سوی is a preposition 'toward/to' marking destination; طیسفون is the place name 'Ctesiphon' as object of سوی; کاردیده is a participial adjective 'experienced, tested' that modifies مهان (guests/visitors), so کاردیده مهان = 'seasoned envoys/visitors'. The sequence is preposition + place + qualifying phrase for the travelers.
segments.s572-g5: بدان here functions as a purpose-marker roughly 'to that end / for that' (written as ب + دان in the segmentation); تا introduces the purpose clause 'so that'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; قیصر is 'the Caesar/emperor'; دهند is a present-subjunctive/jussive plural form of دادن 'they may give/bring' used for reported information; آگهی is 'news/information'. So the clause means 'so that they may bring news from the Caesar.'
segments.s572-g6: ازان = 'from that/thereupon' (referring back to the place); برز is an adjective 'high/exalted'; درگاه is 'court' (here 'royal court' or 'presence'); با = 'with'; فرهی is an adjective/noun 'noble, cultured' (here 'with the nobles' or 'with the noble ones'). The phrase describes the source/location and companions: 'from that exalted court with the noble.'
segments.s572-g7: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/went' (verb-first order is poetic); کارآگهان is the plural subject 'the informed/knowledgeable ones'; ناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly' modifying the action. The sequence shows a poetic inversion (verb before subject) and a final adverb for suddenness.
segments.s572-g8: نهفته is an adjective/noun 'the hidden (things)' serving as the object; بجستند is the past plural of جستن used transitively here meaning 'they sought/sought out'; کار جهان is a genitive construction 'affairs of the world' (کار = affairs/deeds, جهان = world). Word order places the object (نهفته) before the verb for emphasis, then the noun phrase specifying what was sought.
segments.s572-g57: سراپرده قیصر is a possessive noun phrase: 'the inner pavilion/tent of the Caesar.' The adjective بی‌هنر ('unskilled/clumsy') is placed immediately after this phrase for metrical/poetic reasons but semantically modifies the agent (Shapur) in the next clause — it is a postposed predicative adjective describing the manner of the action.
segments.s572-g58: همی کرد شاپور زیر و زبر is the verbal clause: همی is the continuous/emphatic particle ('was/indeed'), کرد is the past third-person plural/singular active verb 'did/made' (here 'overturned'), and شاپور is the agent/name. The phrase زیر و زبر is an idiomatic pair meaning 'upside down / topsy-turvy' formed by زیر ('under') + و + زبر ('over/above'), describing the result of the action.
segments.s572-g59: به هر گوشه‌ای is a prepositional phrase 'into/in every corner' (به = 'into/towards', هر = 'each/every', گوشه‌ای = 'corner'). آتش اندر زدند uses اندر ('in/within') plus زدند (past plural 'they struck/they set'), so the clause means 'they struck/laid fire within every corner' i.e. 'they set fires in every corner.'
segments.s572-g60: همی is the habitual/emphatic particle ('indeed/was'), followed by the compound phrase آسمان بر زمین بر زدند where آسمان ('the sky') is the object/figure being struck and بر زمین ('upon the earth') is the locus; زدند is the past plural 'they struck/smote.' Together this poetic line says they 'struck the sky against the earth' (a vivid image of attack or great upheaval).
segments.s572-g61: سرانجام is an adverb 'at last/finally.' قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar.' گرفتار شد is a passive/perfect-like construction: گرفتـار ('captured/entangled') + شد ('became/was'), so 'was captured' or 'came to be taken.' The clause is simple S V with سرانجام fronted for emphasis.
segments.s572-g62: وزو here introduces the result/association (poetically 'with/from him' — note the lemma list gives an unexpected gloss, but its function is connective). اختر نیک is the noun phrase 'the good/auspicious star' (اختر = 'star', نیک = 'auspicious/well'), and بیزار شد is 'became averse/hostile' (بیزار = 'averse', شد = 'became'). The whole means the favourable star turned against him.
segments.s572-g63: وزان is a connective 'from that/from those' introducing origin or consequence. خیمه‌ها is the locative/source 'the tents,' and نامداران اوی is a noun phrase 'his renowned/illustrious ones' (نامداران = 'renowned ones'; اوی is the archaic possessive/pronoun 'his'). The verb is omitted for brevity/poetry (understood: 'came' or 'were found').
segments.s572-g64: دلیر و گزیده سواران اوی is a compact noun phrase 'his brave and chosen horsemen.' دلیر and گزیده are coordinate adjectives ('brave' and 'selected/chosen'), linked by و; سواران is the head noun 'horsemen/riders'; اوی again marks possession ('his'). Word order is adjective(s) + noun + possessor (poetic/historical form).
segments.s572-g17: نبیند is a present-tense negative form (ن + بیند from دیدن) placed before the subject as is common in poetry: 'does not see'. همی is a continuous/iterative adverb ('continually/ever'); دشمن is the subject 'the enemy'. از هیچ روی is a prepositional phrase 'from no face/side' (هیچ = 'no/any' here, روی = 'face/side') specifying the manner/place the enemy does not see.
segments.s572-g18: پسند آمدش is an impersonal/experiential construction meaning 'it came to him as pleasing' = 'he liked'. آمدش = آمد + ـش ('it came to him'). زیستن is the infinitive 'to live' serving as the object of پسند آمد. برزوی is a proper name/place (Barzū) marking where he liked to live.
segments.s572-g19: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شاپور is the subject. بشنید is the past tense 'heard' (he heard). زان = ز + آن 'from that / of that' refers back to the preceding statement. شاد شد is the two-word predicate 'became happy' (شاد = happy, شد = became).
segments.s572-g20: همه رنجها is the subject NP 'all the sorrows/pains'. بر دلش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his heart' (بر = on, دلش = his heart). باد شد (literally 'became wind') is a poetic metaphor: 'turned to wind' = 'blew away/vanished' ; شد is the past/copular verb 'became'.
segments.s572-g21: گزین کرد is the past-tense verb phrase 'he chose/selected' (گزین = choose, کرد = did/made used as past). ز ایرانیان is a genitive/from-phrase 'from the Iranians'. سه هزار is the direct object/quantity 'three thousand' placed after the prepositional phrase for poetic word order.
segments.s572-g22: This is a coordinated nominal list describing those chosen: زره‌دار 'armored (men)' and then additional riders. و is the conjunction 'and'. برگستوان and ور here function as part of the descriptive phrase before سوار; سوار is 'horseman/rider'. The exact nuance of برگستوان is specialized/archaic (see senses note), while ور has an emphatic/also-like value in older Persian.
segments.s572-g23: شب تیره is the temporal phrase 'a dark night'. جوشن به بر is a light-verb/causative-like phrase meaning 'the cuirass upon (his) body' — جوشن = armor, به بر = onto the chest/body. در کشید here is the verbal phrase 'drew on / put on' (کشید = pulled/drew) used with جوشن به بر to mean 'put on the armor'. Word order is poetic (time — object — verb).
segments.s572-g24: سپه را is the definite object 'the army' marked by را. سوی طیسفون is a directional phrase 'toward Tisphon/Ctesiphon' (سوی = toward, طیسفون = place name). برکشید is the past tense verb 'he drew up/raised/moved' (here 'moved/led' the army toward the city). The verb follows the object and directional phrase for emphasis.
segments.s572-g25: به is the preposition 'towards/into' introducing the following locative phrase. تیره is an adjective 'dark' modifying the noun that follows. شبان here is the noun governed by به (the intended meaning is 'night/night-time' in the poem), but the supplied senses do not include that meaning (see senses). تیز is an adverbial adjective 'swift' placed before the verb to modify it, and بشتافتی is the past second-person singular of بشتافتن ('you hastened/rushed') with the archaic/poetic ending -تی marking 'you (did)'.
segments.s572-g26: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. روشن is predicative 'lit/bright' and is governed by the verb شدی, which is the past second-person of شدن ('you became'). روی is the noun 'face' (the thing concerned), and برتافتی is a past second-person form meaning 'you turned/exposed/appeared' (here: you turned or revealed your face); the temporal clause introduced by چو ('when') sets the time for this action.
segments.s572-g27: همی is the classical habitual/continuous past marker often used in epic narration ('kept/was doing'); راندی is the past second-person singular of راندن ('you drove/rode'). در is the preposition 'in/through' and governs the following coordinated locations بیابان و کوه ('desert and mountain'), which together state the terrain traversed.
segments.s572-g28: بران is a locative/adverbial element meaning 'thereon/thereupon' or 'on that (way)'. راه بی‌راه is a paired expression 'road and un-road' (i.e., both on-road and off-road); the supplied senses for بی‌راه do not include the 'trackless/off-road' reading used here. خود is a emphatic/possessive 'own' or intensifier attached to راه, and با گروه is a prepositional phrase 'with a troop/band' indicating accompaniment.
segments.s572-g29: فزون followed by از expresses 'more than' (an extent beyond); از introduces the quantity 'two parasangs' (فرسنگ). پیش here means 'ahead of' or 'in front of' and links the distance phrase to سپاه ('the army'). The whole phrase gives the distance by which the lookout was forward of the army.
segments.s572-g30: همی again marks the habitual/continuous past and is used with the copula بود ('was') to state a continuing situation. دیده‌بان is the subject 'lookout/sentry', and بود is the past copula 'was' (third-person). بی‌راه و راه is a coordinate phrase 'off-road and road' describing where the lookout was posted; the specific 'off-road' sense for بی‌راه is not among the supplied senses (see senses).
segments.s572-g31: چنین is a discourse adverb 'thus/so' linking to what precedes. تا here is the limit marker 'as far as/until', followed by به نزدیکی ('to the vicinity of') where به is the preposition and نزدیکی is the noun 'nearness/vicinity'; طیسفون is the place name 'Ctesiphon' serving as the endpoint of the motion.
segments.s572-g32: طلایه is the subject 'vanguard/forefront'. همی again gives the continuous/habitual past flavor. راند is the past (third-person) form of راندن 'drove/rode (on)' (note the lack of the -ی ending marks third-person here). پیش اندرون is a locative phrase: پیش 'in front of/ahead' and اندرون 'the inner area/inner quarters', indicating that the vanguard advanced toward the interior.
segments.s572-g33: به + لشکر گه forms a locative prepositional phrase ('to/at the army-camp') — note لشکرگه is a compound 'laskar-gāh' (camp) split here by the preposition. آمد is the perfect/past verb 'came'. گذشته is a past participle/adjective placed after the verb in poetic word-order and modifies دو پاس (two sentries) — literally 'two sentries who had gone/advanced'. The clause as a whole: 'two sentries came to the camp.'
segments.s572-g34: ز marks the relation 'of/from' (here 'of' or 'concerning' Caesar). نبودش is the past copula نبود with the enclitic -ش referring back to the person ('he/it did not have / there was not for him'); here it functions like 'he had no...'. به دل در هراس is a locative phrase 'in (his) heart, in fear' (به دل = 'in the heart', در هراس = 'in fear'). Together: 'he had no fear of Caesar in his heart.'
segments.s572-g35: ازان مرز = 'from that border/frontier' (ازان = 'from that'). بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. آواز کوس is a noun phrase 'the voice/sound of the drum' (آواز + کوس). Word order is typical classical Persian: locative + verb + object.
segments.s572-g36: غو پاسبانان is a genitival/possessive structure 'the tumult/noise of the guards' (غو = uproar, پاسبانان = guards). چو is the comparative conjunction 'like/as'. بانگ خروس = 'the cry/crying of the rooster' (بانگ = cry/sound, خروس = rooster). The clause compares the guards' uproar to a cock's crow.
segments.s572-g37: پر از خیمه is the predicative phrase 'full of tents' (پر = 'full (of)', followed by از + noun). یک دشت و خرگاه is the compounded subject 'a plain and a (low) camp/stall'. بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause reads: 'a plain and camp were full of tents.'
segments.s572-g38: ازان = 'of that/from that'; تاختن is a verbal noun (masdar) meaning 'the charge/charging'. خود here is emphatic 'his/its own' (or 'very/itself'), and که + آگاه + بود is a relative clause 'which he was aware of.' Word-for-word: 'of that charging of his, which he was aware.' The clause explains why the tents were filled/why they acted — he knew of that charge.
segments.s572-g39: ز before a noun here gives the causal sense 'because of/since.' می‌مست is an adjectival/participle form meaning 'intoxicated' (with wine). قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar/emperor.' به پرده‌سرای is a locative phrase 'in the curtain-chamber/curtained apartment' (به = 'in/at' + پرده‌سرای). The whole segment: 'Since the Caesar was intoxicated in the curtained apartment...' (classical word order places the cause up front).
segments.s572-g40: ز لشکر here functions to mark the relation 'for the army' or 'as to the army' (ز often carries 'of/for' in poetry). نبود is the past negative/existential 'there was not' (past). اندران = اندر + ان, here 'in/among that' or 'therein'; مرز جای = 'a place in the border' (مرز = border, جای = place). Together: 'there was no place for the army in that frontier.'
segments.s572-g41: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. گیتی چنان is the object phrase 'the world thus' placed before the verb. دید is a past‑tense 3rd‑person singular verb 'saw'. The subject شاپور گرد follows the verb (classical Persian often places subject after the verb); here گرد is part of the name/epithet attached to شاپور rather than a separate preposition.
segments.s572-g42: عنان کیی is a possessive/attributive phrase meaning 'the Kayanian reins' (کیی = 'Kayanian' follows and qualifies عنان). بارگی را is the direct object (را marks the person Bargī as what is given/entrusted). سپرد is the past verb 'entrusted / handed over' that takes بارگی را as its object; word order is possessee (عنان کیی) + object (بارگی را) + verb (سپرد).
segments.s572-g43: سپه را is the definite object 'the army' marked by را. به لشکرگه اندر is a prepositional phrase 'into the camp' (به ... اندر = 'to/into ...'). کشید is a past tense verb 'drew/led/dragged' governing that object and prepositional phrase; here it means 'moved/led (the army) into'.
segments.s572-g44: بزد is past 'he struck / smote'. دست functions instrumentally here ('struck with his hand') even though no separate instrumental preposition appears — a common classical usage. و simply links the two actions. گرز گران is the noun + adjective 'heavy mace' (adjective after noun). برکشید is the compound verb 'lifted/raised up' (بر کشیدن) in the past tense, describing the second action.
segments.s572-g45: به ابر اندر آمد is a locative clause 'came into the cloud(s)' (به ... اندر = 'into/within'). آمد is past 'came'. دم کرنای is a genitive construction 'the blast (دم) of the horn (کرنای)' with the possessor following the possessed noun (classical ordering).
segments.s572-g46: جرنگیدن is a verbal noun (masdar) meaning 'the clattering/clang' — here used as a noun ('the clattering of ...'). It is followed by its genitive complements: گرز (mace) and then هندی درای (an Indian‑qualified noun phrase). و links the two items in the genitive. The exact nuance of درای in this line is obscure here; syntactically هندی modifies درای.
segments.s572-g47: دهاده is a quantitative/adverbial word 'many' or 'tenfold' that modifies the following verb. برآمد is past 'arose/appeared'. ز هر پهلوی is a prepositional phrase 'from every flank/side' (ز = 'from', هر = 'every', پهلوی = 'flank/side').
segments.s572-g48: چکاچاک is an onomatopoeic noun/adverb meaning the clinking/clattering sound; here it functions as the thing that 'arose' or 'sprang up'. برخاست is past 'rose / sprang up'. از هر سوی is a locative phrase 'from every direction/quarter' (از = 'from', هر سوی = 'every side').
segments.s572-g49: تو گفتی همی = 'you said/kept saying' (subject + 2nd-person past verb). همی is a poetic particle marking iterative or emphatic speech here ('kept saying' / 'kept insisting'). آسمان بترکید is reported speech following گفتی: آسمان (subject) + بترکید (past verb 'burst'); the initial b- is an archaic/poetic verbal prefix seen in epic diction.
segments.s572-g50: ز خورشید خون بر هوا برچکید = causal/source phrase + action: ز + خورشید = 'from/because of the sun'; خون 'blood' is the affected substance; بر هوا uses بر as spatial preposition 'upon/in the air'; برچکید is a past-form verb meaning 'sprang forth / gushed' (بر- prefix giving a sense of forthness). The whole clause describes the violent image that was being reported.
segments.s572-g51: درفشیدن کاویانی درفش = a compressed nominal phrase where درفشیدن functions as a verbal noun ('the unfurling/raising') and کاویانی modifies درفش ('the Kayanian banner'). Poetic word order compresses the genitive/attributive relation: literally 'the unfurling of the Kayanian banner.'
segments.s572-g52: شب تیره و تیغهای بنفش = two coordinated nominal images 'a dark night and purple swords.' تیره follows شب as an adjective (night dark), and بنفش follows تیغهای as adjective after the plural noun (swords purple). The construction lists vivid battlefield imagery in apposition to the banner.
segments.s572-g53: تو گفتی هوا تیغ بارد همی = 'you said: the air rains blades.' تو گفتی again introduces reported speech. Here هوا is the grammatical subject, تیغ the thing 'raining' from it, and بارد is the verbal form (from باریدن 'to fall/rain' with poetic b- prefix) meaning 'rains/falls' — همی adds iterative/emphatic aspect. The clause is verbless in modern order but in epic Persian the verb follows its complements.
segments.s572-g54: جهان یکسره میغ دارد همی = 'the world wholly has blades' → 'the whole world is full of blades.' جهان (subject) + یکسره (adverb 'entirely') modifies the predication; میغ 'blade' is the possessed item, دارد is the present-tense verb 'has' (literal possession used idiomatically to mean 'is full of'), and همی again adds emphatic/continuous colouring.
segments.s572-g55: ز گرد سپه کوه شد ناپدید = prepositional phrase (ز گرد سپه 'from the dust of the host/army') followed by the result clause کوه شد ناپدید 'the mountain became invisible.' شد (past/become) + predicative adjective ناپدید expresses a change of state: the mountain was hidden by the army's dust/host.
segments.s572-g56: ستاره همی دامن اندرکشید = 'the star drew in its hem/skirt.' ستاره (subject) + همی (emphatic/continuous particle) + دامن (direct object, here a poetic image for 'skirt/hem [of light or the heavens]') + اندرکشید (past verb 'drew in' with اندر- indicating inward motion). The clause personifies the star withdrawing its radiance.
segments.s572-g65: گرفتند is past-tense, 3rd-person plural ('they seized/took'); بسیار here functions as an object/adverbial meaning 'many' (i.e. 'many [people/things]'). و simply coordinates the two verbs. کردند is the second past 3rd-person plural ('they made/did'), agreeing with the same subject; بند is the noun 'fetter/bond' as the object of کردند. The clause compactly expresses two actions by the same implied subject: 'they seized many and made (them) bonds/ fetters.'
segments.s572-g66: چنین است is a fixed copular phrase 'thus is/so it is' (چنین = 'thus', است = 'is'). کردار is the noun 'deed/action' as the predicative noun. چرخ بلند ('the high/lofty Wheel') follows without the expected ezâfe (a common poetic omission) and functions as the genitive/agentive modifier of کردار: 'the deed of the lofty Wheel' (i.e. fate/heaven).
segments.s572-g67: گهی at clause start = 'sometimes.' ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (z + pronoun 'it' = 'from it'), referring back to 'the Wheel' (fate). فراز آید is an impersonal/simple present/presentive sense 'a rising comes' (آید = 'comes'/'happens'). و links the contrast, and in گه نشیب the verb آید is omitted by ellipsis and گه ('sometimes') qualifies نشیب ('a descent'); so the structure contrasts rising and descent as alternating occurrences.
segments.s572-g68: گهی again = 'sometimes.' شادمانی is a noun 'joy' and is coordinated with the following element by و. گاهی is a variant adverb 'sometimes' used here for parallelism, and نهیب is a noun 'rebuke' (or 'threatening cry'); the verb (آید) is omitted again, so the clause lists alternating outcomes: 'sometimes joy, and sometimes rebuke.'
segments.s572-g69: بی‌آزاری is a nominalized adjective meaning 'harmlessness/being harmless.' و coordinates the two qualities. مردمی is used adjectivally/nominatively here to indicate 'toward the people' or 'of the people' (poetically functioning as 'kindness to the people' or 'being among/for the people'). بهترست is the copular + comparative 'is better' (archaic contracted form of بهتر است), predicated of the coordinated nouns.
segments.s572-g70: کرا is an archaic/interrogative-relative pronoun meaning 'who/whom' or 'for whom' (classical Persian usage). کردگار جهان 'the Maker/Creator of the world' is the noun phrase (no ezâfe shown in poetic compression). یاورست is 'is helper' (copula + predicate), so the inverted order yields the sense 'for whom the Maker of the world is a helper' — i.e. 'he whom God helps' or 'he who has God as his ally.'

sentences.s572-s5: They saw it and returned by every route, then came before the proud king.
sentences.s572-s6: for (the) Caesar, from drinking wine and from the hunt, thought nothing at all about battle.
sentences.s572-s7: His army was scattered from every side, to plunder into every flank.
sentences.s572-s8: Neither did he have a vanguard by day nor a guard by night; his whole army was like a flock without a shepherd.
sentences.s572-s1: In those days it was rare that six thousand men should form an army.
sentences.s572-s2: Shapur sent informed men toward Ctesiphon — seasoned guests/visitors.
sentences.s572-s3: They were sent so that they might bring news of the Caesar from that exalted court, with the noble (ones).
sentences.s572-s4: The informed men set out suddenly and sought the hidden affairs of the world.
sentences.s572-s29: Shapur, clumsily, overturned the Caesar's inner pavilion.
sentences.s572-s30: They set fire in every corner; they smote the sky upon the earth.
sentences.s572-s31: At last the Caesar was captured, and with him his auspicious star turned hostile.
sentences.s572-s32: From those tents came his renowned men — his brave, chosen horsemen.
sentences.s572-s9: The enemy saw him from no side; it pleased him to live at Barzū.
sentences.s572-s10: When Shapur heard that, he rejoiced; all his sufferings became like wind upon his heart.
sentences.s572-s11: He selected three thousand from the Iranians: armored men and (other) mounted horsemen.
sentences.s572-s12: On a dark night he put on his cuirass and led the host toward Ctesiphon.
sentences.s572-s13: You rushed swiftly through the dark night; when you were lit up you turned your face.
sentences.s572-s14: You rode on through desert and over mountain, on both roads and trackless ways with your band.
sentences.s572-s15: A lookout stood more than two parasangs ahead of the army, on both road and off-road.
sentences.s572-s16: Thus, up to the vicinity of Ctesiphon the vanguard drove on before the inner quarters.
sentences.s572-s17: Two sentries came up to the army camp; there was no fear of the Caesar in his heart.
sentences.s572-s18: From that frontier he heard the sound of the war-drum; the guards' tumult was like the crowing of a rooster.
sentences.s572-s19: A plain and a lowly encampment were full of tents, for he was aware of that very charge.
sentences.s572-s20: Because the Caesar was drunk in the curtained chamber, there was no place for the army within that border.
sentences.s572-s21: When Shapur Gard saw the world thus, he entrusted the Kayanian reins to Bargī.
sentences.s572-s22: He drew the army into the camp; he struck with his hand and lifted a heavy mace.
sentences.s572-s23: Into the clouds came the blast of the horn; the clatter of maces and (of) the Indian ... resounded.
sentences.s572-s24: Many (tenfold) rose from every flank; clinking sprang up from every quarter.
sentences.s572-s25: You kept saying, “The sky has burst; from the sun blood gushed into the air.”
sentences.s572-s26: The Kayanian banner was unfurled: a dark night and purple swords.
sentences.s572-s27: You said, 'The air will rain blades; the whole world is entirely edged with swords.'
sentences.s572-s28: From the army's dust the mountain became invisible; the star drew in its skirt.
sentences.s572-s33: They seized many and bound them; such is the deed of the lofty Wheel.
sentences.s572-s34: At times a rising comes from it, and at times a descent; sometimes joy and sometimes a rebuke.
sentences.s572-s35: Harmlessness and kindness toward the people are better; whom the Maker of the world helps (i.e. he who has God as helper).
